speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three, and we are with Regina [? Weber. ?] And it's the 10th of March, 1995. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about Auschwitz, Regina. We were talking about how the count was every morning, and how your day went. Did you have a job?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody had jobs. Nobody had jobs. It was-- we were there to be killed, to become thin, called a musselman. And if you had a red spot on you, they thought that it was typhoid. And they took you away. And every day they took people away to be gassed and cremated.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew that they were being gassed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. By that time we knew. And you lived-- fear is not a word. We were running, running for our life all the time. We-- I guess we didn't want to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: We didn't want to go into the gas chamber. So we-- you know, we-- all five of us were always together, you know? And at the end of the summer, coming to fall already-- they-- Was it getting cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: Getting cold. And they kept on taking-- they still took out the healthier looking ones and took them to other camps. And we were running, even, up until the end of November, where we just have a very few people left. We couldn't run anywhere. And they got us, the five sisters and whoever was left over.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were left do you think, roughly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from the thousands--

speaker: interviewer
text: In your camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: --and thousands, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's very difficult for me to say. You couldn't tell.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because many of them were killed. Many of them were taken to other camps where they worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't go until the end of November. There was like this last clearing of the lager. And we had to stay in line.

speaker: interviewer
text: November, 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: November, 1944. And we went through, in that cold and everything, that say our appell. And we were taken to the life side. And there was a friend of ours, a young woman was taken to the left side. And her mother got out of the line and went to the left side with her daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a friend of ours. I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: How could you forget that?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was a lovely, lovely intelligent girl. And her mother went with her to die. And all five of us got in to the life side. And we-- somehow we stole outside, so a coat. And I had two different shoes on. And they sent us out in front of the crematorium. We stood all night long. And we said, this is it. They're going to put us into the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the five of you they sent--

speaker: interviewee
text: No!

speaker: interviewer
text: --out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I mean, maybe a few hundred people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of--

speaker: interviewee
text: 200 people or 300, or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stood in front of the crematorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, kind of trying to give up. I don't know, you just become-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --insane, you know, with fear. And we stood there hours and hours, freezing. And towards when morning broke, they took us into those cattle cars and took us to another work camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Birnbaumel. If you want, I can--

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell that please.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. B-I-R-N-B-A-U-M-E-L. And we had arrived there, and there was another German Oberscharfuhrer with the dog. And you know, with-- we were surrounded by wires, you know? And all of a sudden--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a work camp, Regina?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a work camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to dig ditches for the German Army. They called them [GERMAN], which means ditches.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this in Poland, or was this--

speaker: interviewee
text: I imagine it was in Poland, you know? Or Silesia, which was-- or something close to, you know, Poland, or in Poland, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how long the trip took from Auschwitz to Birnbaumel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, Three nights, three days, something like that. But don't hold me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --for it, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was winter already. And they put us into a thin wood circular barrack with one little heater in the center. And early in the morning, you had to go out and dig the ditches.

speaker: interviewee
text: But because I was so little and there were two other kids who were little like me, my sister, Izabel, went over to the Oberscharfuhrer. And she says, why don't you let them stay in the camp and let them clean the toilets? Which weren't toilets. They were just holes. You know? Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: They agreed?

speaker: interviewee
text: They agreed. And us little-- three little kids, who were very friendly to each other, for 24 hours a day, we talked about food. That's all we did. Our mind couldn't function anyway. Three little orphans, you know, talking about food. And we stayed there till--

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't have to give me exact dates.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me what--

speaker: interviewee
text: January 23rd.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they got us together, and we had to go on a forced march in the snow. And we heard some noises like they're taking us to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So January '45. This is the--

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, the war is just over.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't over yet. I think it wasn't over till May.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. It was over before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: But anyway, go ahead. You--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, they-- they took us on this forced march. And one night they put us in a farm. And we ate frozen-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some kind of vegetable, frozen--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --or something. And my sister, [? Chicha, ?] has always been a very unusually bright person. And she got us together. And she said, listen, we stood together this long. We can't anymore. To each his own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to-- we cannot stay together anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think she said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: She heard some-- maybe some, you know, fighting or-- or bullets or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aircraft.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she figured out-- not aircraft This was real fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that the Russians must be close, or the Germans are coming back. We always thought, well, the Germans are coming back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next day, which was the 24th of January, we're marching.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you wearing-- do you have boots?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- my feet-- my feet were frozen, my little toes. And it was unbelievably cold and icy and snowy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how many people were on this march, roughly?

speaker: interviewer
text: 300. And who--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd say.

speaker: interviewer
text: --who's on the side? Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans with the dogs and some-- some Jews who were there five years and became animals. And my thought was all the time while we were marching, if we's remain alive, I am going to kill that Jewish woman. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why-- why not the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't. They were armed, you know? But it hurt so much that they made an animal out of this poor Jewish woman. I still had feelings for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's a kapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: A kapo. And my mind, I remember, was totally occupied, that I'm going to tear her apart. Because she made us go! Go! Go! Go! Go!

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she Hungarian or Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think Polish, but don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're not sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure, so I don't want to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --say, against anybody--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And anyway, we are marching. And [? Chicha ?] is here. Then I am here. Then Izabel. And then [? Cipi, ?] my oldest sister. And it's daylight. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? --Chicha ?] figured out something. She ran out of the line. And she saw a house, and she went in there. And I didn't think. I ran out. And Izabel didn't think. And she ran out. And my oldest sister didn't run. She couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yolan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yolan, [? Cipi, ?] she remained with that horrible line.

speaker: interviewer
text: And nobody shot at you? Nobody was shooting at you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were busy, I think, shooting at somebody at the other end. And [? Chicha, ?] we didn't see. And Izabel and I hear that dog in this house bark. And I said, Izabel, let's go in the front. They're coming after us anyway. They'll catch us. And we didn't go. We went to the back of the house. And eventually, they passed us by. It's a miracle.

speaker: interviewer
text: The line just kept going.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kept going. And the oberscharfuhrer didn't come for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: She didn't notice?

speaker: interviewee
text: He.

speaker: interviewer
text: He didn't notice.

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't notice. And then they were gone, maybe a half an hour, Izabel and I said, well, let's go into this house. We don't know where [? Chicha ?] is. We go in the house. And there were already two Polish women in there. And we say, [? Chicha-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Two Polish Jewish women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who also, I guess, got out of the line. And [? Chicha ?] is-- we call her name, and she was in the attic. So we go up there.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, you know, there are no people here. The smoke is still going. They must have just evacuated this village. Smart as anything. I couldn't think in those terms, because I was normal, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she figured it out. And we're in a home that has everything in it, all the food that you ever saw in your life, no people. Then when we were able to go out of the house, we saw the whole village is totally evacuated.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans pushed the people ahead, that they shouldn't be caught. And they left all the food and everything. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: We started to eat. And we got very sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first thing you ate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think they had pasta. They had bread. They had everything from all over the world. And then I saw chickens and a duck and a goose. And I, my little frightened self, go out and found something, and I killed one of the-- goose or something. And I my made chicken soup. And we got so sick from it because it had fat in it, and we couldn't digest anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got very sick. And we were very thin anyway. So when we had this diarrhea, we just became really very sick. And you-- you were in-- you continued to stay in this house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the other two polish women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But the next day, the 25th--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is January.

speaker: interviewee
text: --We look out the window, and there is in an army coming. First we heard, you know, boom, boom. With a red flag. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And it was the Russian Army. And they didn't really stop. And we were not-- we had some kind of number on our neck. And we said, we're Jewish. You know, we couldn't speak Russian. And they left us alone, and they went.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, good. Did you ever get numbers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. There--

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: --was no time. When--

speaker: interviewer
text: They--

speaker: interviewee
text: --my part of Hungary came, we just went to the gas chambers. There was--

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't bother with the numbers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was no time for it. They gave us a little paper something. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Russians were the liberating army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, they were coming through this part of Upper Silesia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister, [? Chicha, ?] figured this out. When she heard the boom, boom, she says, the Russians must be behind the Germans, or ahead of them, that-- she figured it out. She was very bright. And then-- [SIGHS] --trouble came.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian soldiers would come back at night and look at us. And I guess they wanted to-- what do you call that? Attack us physically.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rape. So we started to run and hide in basements and attics, everywhere again. It was horrendous.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were running from the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russians. I got dressed as a little boy because I wanted them to think that I was a little boy. And it's unbelievable, but one night, a Russian soldier came in and said, [NON-ENGLISH] to my sister, [? Chicha. ?] And he lied down in between two women. And there was an older married woman who took-- take-- changed with my sister, Chicha. And she wasn't raped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other woman-- I mean, things like this don't happen every day. It's just horrendous. But then the Russians, you know, didn't have much food. And they made us stay in this little village somewhere. And we had to take care of there-- there was some animals, which they wanted to take away. And we had to learn how to milk the animals--

speaker: interviewer
text: Milk the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And running at night. Worried they're going to get in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you were under Russian domination?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it wasn't like the German?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they weren't going to kill us. But to us--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel safer at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and no. Because to an Orthodox Jewish girl who's never had sex, the fear of that was horrendous. And yet, we found some nice Russians, the younger ones, who understand-- understood and said, we'll stay with you tonight, or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: We'll watch you, you know? It's amazing, but there were some young people who kind of took us under their wing. But it was terribly frightening, terribly. So the three of you were still together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three of us together. And we still didn't know what's going to happen to us, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because after a while, the Russians didn't have any more food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, go, go, go. And everybody who was liberated, there were army-- American army men, from Israel, from India, the whole world was marching from over Silesia. Two weeks we were marching. And somehow, somebody said, we're going to a place called [? Oelsk. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's O-E-L-S-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or just O-L-S-K. Because there is a train, and it's going to take us somewhere. And there was no food anyway. We had to go. And you know, you met people from all over that were prisoners in Germany, or working in Germany, and an army marching in this cold weather. And two weeks of walking, we arrived in [? Oelsk. ?] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in-- still in Silesia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, in Upper Silesia, near [? Proschwitz, ?] or near somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been, still, Poland. And they-- [SIGHS] We arrived there. And they kind of, the Russians, separated each nationality. And some they sent back to Budapest. Some, the American Army people, they held together. And everybody was separated. And I think we had a place to stay at. It was a-- a concentration-- not a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But where they wanted to send these people back to their homes because they didn't have food to feed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: But--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they wanted them to go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: All under the auspices of the Russian Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian-- Russian Army. One night, what we decided, the three of us, we are not going back to Hungary. We are not going back to a cemetery. We know my mother and little sister, and maybe my oldest sister, were killed. And we don't want to go back to our grave. We don't want to--

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no idea--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --what happened to him. But we were always hopeful. Phillip is going to remain alive. We knew that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because he was one of these leaders, one of-- one of these terrific young men, you know? And we never thought of anything else but Phillip is going to be alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: But at this point, we decided, the three of us, we are Americans. My father has been in America already from '40 to '45, five years. And we are Americans. We are not going back to Hungary. And one night-- you know, we were only there a couple of nights-- the Russian soldiers come and they say, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. The three American sisters, come with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took us to the American attache.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had told the Russians that you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh--

speaker: interviewer
text: --Americans.

speaker: interviewee
text: --we're Americans. You know, there was no way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody speak English?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not one word. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: But the attache heard about us, so he got an English flier who spoke Yiddish. And we spoke Yiddish to him. And we told our whole story to the attache. And he almost cried.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aw.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, I never had such an experience. Right away he gave us chocolate. And he says, I don't have [? wax ?] clothing. But I have little soldiers clothing, and I want you to get in it. And he said, you know, this was in [? Oelsk, ?] or was this in Odessa already even?

speaker: interviewer
text: It doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, the point is, I think that we got with the train to Odessa, and this happened in Odessa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're wearing American uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's cute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah, it was very cute. We were very proud of it, you know? And wonderful food, and-- anyway, I think this was already at Odessa, where the American Army attache. And he said to us, now you say you're Americans, but we don't really know. But I believe you three orphans. And I'm going to investigate. I guess he got in touch with the FBI. And they got, right away, touch with my father. And they knew that we were alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said-- he said, I tell you what I'm going to do for you. There are 30 Liberty ships in Odessa here, which brought armaments to Russia. And we-- I'm going to try to get on one of those ships, even you have no papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's going to try to get the three girls onto one of those ships, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: The three girls. And he went next day, and none of the Liberty ships want to take women. So he said to them, if you don't think them, the other one will take them. And he got us on a Liberty ship, which was a convoy of 30 ships. And the war was still going on at January--

speaker: interviewee
text: --something, January '45. And-- oh, but by this time it's not January. Because we went-- we were with the Russians for six weeks. Then we March for two weeks to [? Oelsk. ?] Then we took the train to Odessa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's already April 5th. We were on the road, trying to get to America, not to Hungary. We saw Hungarian kids going back with  their trains to Hungary. But we said, that's not for us. You didn't-- you didn't want to look for your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we knew that he wouldn't go back to Hungary either.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I mean, we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard what happened to your older sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not until the war was over, and we were here for awhile. No, no, no, no. Nobody could tell us anything. Anyway, so we had this hearing, you know, with this American attache. And he got us on a boat. And it was unbelievable how they treated us there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the food-- and-- and we started to learn English the second day, you know? Because we had to order food. We were with the captain in a beautiful dining room. And then we were treated like officers, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And one of the officers was a Jewish fellow, Jack Berman. And we were standing on the bottom of the boat. He told this person of the ship, he says, you see that girl? I want that girl to stay at my table at the dining room. What girl was he pointing at?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chicha. She was never good looking, but she had some femininity, unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, she's the one who had all the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Brains.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the brains, you're telling me?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the brains, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we were on that boat that was called the Brand Whitlock. Maybe I don't have to spell it for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sounds wonderful. No you don't.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were treated like queens. And my brother-in-law spoke Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he became your brother-in-law. You didn't tell us yet. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're going ahead of yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: But Jack, Jack Berman--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --was one of the officers on the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we arrived, two weeks later, he married my sister, [? Chicha. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow, that's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, the point is though, that when we arrived in this country, on May 8th, 1945, I think the war was over May 8th or May 10th. And they took us to like, an hearing room again with the FBI. And they had Hungarian interpreters. And we had to tell our whole story. And we didn't have any papers. And because they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where your father was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We have the address. And somehow, they let us into this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: We-- because we were, I think, the first ones from Auschwitz to come and tell them the truth, what happened there. And because they really saw that my father is living in this country, they let us into my father's-- what is it? Cognisance or something, you know? He was like the guardian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Guardian, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's a miraculous story. Did you meet your father after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How soon after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just another day. Because they first took us to Baltimore with the ship. And there the Jewish Women's Council took care of us at a beautiful house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my father came up there. And when he saw in men's uniforms, he got purple in his face. Because he didn't want his beautiful, young daughters to be like men, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And trouble started immediately with my father. First of all, he accused us and me, you killed your mother because you got away from religion. Because I said to my father, you know, there is no God in this world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because God would not ever looked down on Auschwitz and let that happen to us. And how does a little rotter has the nerve to say this to her father? But I was so angry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he said we killed our mother by not being religious. He was a very stubborn man, and so were we. Coming from Auschwitz, you're just not going to do this to us. And we were waiting for a few weeks. And an-- and an American soldier wrote us a letter that found my brother in Feldafing. He was shot on the leg, but he's all right. And you're going to hear from him. So we just took that for granted. Because we felt, all throughout, my brother is going to remain alive. Because he wants the world to know about this. And he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: To be a witness.

speaker: interviewee
text: To be a witness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was-- from the moment he was liberated, he was working on Jewish programs, and organized everything in Feldafing. And he was just-- he's still a very bright fellow and very good. He's very much like my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's just giving. His whole life is giving to the Jewish people. And-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you stay with your father? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, let's see. We arrived in May, until the fall, September. And that's my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Must have been hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was unbelievably hard, because we just fought. And you know, 50 years later, now, I can say to myself, I don't blame my father. He couldn't do anything than what he did. He had to let us go. And we had to leave him. Because Auschwitz made these kind of non-believe-- non-believing people out of us. And my father couldn't accept that. But this wasn't in 1945. This was already years later, that I came to this conclusion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Our tape is-- time is running up. And we're going to go to our next tape in a minute or two. And we're going to talk about your life today--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and your daughter, and how you met your husband. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't think I could--
